note: Auditable view-coverage decision table for the 3-foot backyard trebuchet (task TREB). Each candidate area is scored on (1) installation/fabrication complexity and (2) difficulty of seeing it from the primary whole-model views, then decided ZOOM or a recorded WHY-NOT. Audited BOTH directions: a high-complexity, poorly-visible area with no zoom and no why-not is a finding; a redundant zoom (mirror/simple/well-visible) is over-generation and is a why-not here. Renders: scripts/render_trebuchet_views.py -> outputs/trebuchet/views/. DRAWDIM (naive-builder review follow-up): three derived 2D SHEETS added — every rectangle a placed part's bbox, every number read from the compiled namespace, nothing hand-typed.
context_treatment: The ground is a role:existing context slab sized to show the machine's stance plus the launch lane's start; the hero iso and all zooms hide it. The pivot's occlusion problem (the arm slab hides the hardware between the uprights) is solved by role-splitting the two pivot zooms: z_pivot hides the ARM to show the rod/nut/washer clamp stacks; z_arm hides the UPRIGHTS to show the arm riding the rod between its thrust stacks (finding V1).

primary_views.shows: whole machine on the ground from the launch side, level-arm reference pose
THE mechanism elevation: 32in axle height, 4:1 arm split, runway, gusset knees
down the throw axis: the 14in counterweight lane between the uprights
the 2x4 ladder base, runway plate, and the arm over the centerline

candidate_areas.area: whole machine form (braced posts, ladder base, hung arm)
candidate_areas.rationale: with the context slab hidden the frame's construction logic reads in one image: rails, crosses, runway, uprights, knees, rod, arm. Leads the document.

candidate_areas.area: pivot assembly — rod nutted to both uprights (washer+nut inside and out)
candidate_areas.rationale: the machine's most load-critical, most occluded assembly: from every primary the arm slab and the uprights hide the eight-piece nut/washer stack. With the arm hidden the rod's full span, both upright bores' positions and the floating thrust cluster read. The hardware is small at frame scale — the caption carries the stack order (finding V2).

candidate_areas.area: arm riding the rod between thrust washers + jam pairs
candidate_areas.rationale: the pivot's other half: the arm's bore on the rod shank flanked by fender-washer thrust faces and double jam nuts. The 1/16in hanging tangency is asserted by a dimension check, not visible at render scale — the caption says so honestly (finding V3).

candidate_areas.area: base corner joint cluster: upright lap + gusset knee + cross member butt
candidate_areas.rationale: three different joint words meet in one 12in cube: the upright face-lapped to the rail (screws driven from the rail side), the ply knee lapping the upright's inner face, and a cross member butt-screwed through the rail. One zoom shows all three layers.

candidate_areas.area: the mirror (-X) base corner and pivot hardware
candidate_areas.rationale: mirror-identical about the Y-Z plane; one representative zoom per subject suffices — more would be over-generation.

candidate_areas.area: governing mechanism dims (32in axle, 4:1 split, 14in lane)
candidate_areas.rationale: dimensioned by the derived callouts on the SIDE primary + asserted in the model's dimension checks + guarded by the e2e test; a dedicated zoom adds nothing the callouts do not. REVISED (builder review): callouts on a shaded view under-carried the numbers a builder tapes. D1 is a dedicated dimensioned elevation: stations chained from one datum, bore stations, screw map, launch direction.

candidate_areas.area: runway-to-cross rail_cap joints and the runway slide surface
candidate_areas.rationale: flat plate screwed down onto flat crosses (six screws, countersunk flush per the connection's fabrication note); the TOP primary and z_lap's runway edge show it, and the seats are proven by three declared bearings. Nothing complex to draw.

candidate_areas.area: counterweight bucket, rope hitch, sling, pouch, release pin, trigger
candidate_areas.rationale: the RIGGING is deliberately unmodeled (no soft-goods vocabulary — recorded work order) and cannot be drawn from the model without faking geometry; the doc's rigging section carries the full recipe, dimensions and tuning instead. An honest render limitation, not a coverage gap. REVISED: the rigging still cannot be modeled, but D3 now DRAWS it as clearly-dashed declared schematic (bucket, sling path, pouch start, trigger eyes) so attachment positions live on paper, not only in prose.

candidate_areas.area: cocked pose / swing envelope / counterweight clearance arc
candidate_areas.rationale: REVISED by the naive-builder review: the trigger/release/pouch positions are defined by the cocked pose, and prose-only positions left a fresh builder at trial-and-error. D3 draws the cocked pose as DERIVED ARITHMETIC over the modeled geometry (arm rotated about the modeled axle to a declared 2in tip clearance) with the rigging as the declared recipe, banner-labeled schematic + kinematics NOT ANALYZED — where things go, proving nothing.

candidate_areas.area: axle bores in the uprights and arm (the three designed FEATUREs)
candidate_areas.rationale: each bore is occupied by the rod in every honest view (z_pivot shows the rod crossing both uprights at the bore stations; z_arm shows the arm's bore region); an empty-bore view would require hiding the rod to show a hole. Bore fit is VALIDATED by the through-hole probe; the cut plan carries each bore's own fabrication step.

candidate_areas.area: pivot hardware stack order + threading sequence
candidate_areas.rationale: builder review: the stack order was validated data + caption prose; assembly needs it as a picture. D2 draws every washer/nut/arm at its modeled position with the threading order spelled out.
